input.background: Exercise 11.11 calculated the mean length of menstrual cycles in an SRS of $n=9$ women. The data revealed $\bar{x}=27.78$ days with standard deviation $s=2.906$ days.
input.data: Exercise 11.11 ：Menstrual cycle lengths (days) in an SRS of nine women are as follows: $\{31,28,26,24,29,33,25,26,28\}$. Use this data to test whether mean menstrual cycle length differs significantly from a lunar month. (A lunar month is 29.5 days.) Assume population values vary according to a Normal distribution. Use a twosided alternative. Show all hypothesis-testing steps.
input.question: Calculate a $95 \%$ confidence interval for the mean menstrual cycle length.

output.answer: The confidence interval can be calculated using the formula $\bar{x} \pm t^* \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$, where $t^*$ is the critical value from the t-distribution for $df=n-1$ at $95 \%$ confidence level.

menstrual_cycle_lengths.description: Lengths of menstrual cycles in days for a sample of nine women.

sample_mean.description: Sample mean of the menstrual cycle lengths.

sample_standard_deviation.description: Standard deviation of the menstrual cycle lengths in the sample.

sample_size.description: Number of women in the sample.

confidence_level.description: Confidence level for the confidence interval calculation.
